author: [SAI] Urto666
content: Yes, without current cash flows included and at current burn rate. Also the team was likely very close to cash flow positive this last month IMO, so as I said the runway keeps getting pushed out. Note: These are my personal estimations. It was only since March that I believe the team had not been cash flow positive so they have barely dipped into their cash reserves that they have had for the last two years. The team had 12+ months of runway at the end of July 2023 in the worst case scenario I believe, they dipped into it in Sept & Oct 2023 and then from Nov 2023 to March 2024 the team was cash flow positive with some months earning well over a months burn rate. So I would assume a few extra months of runway were added in the good times and then in March and April and very little in May 2025 the team dipped into this cash reserve.  This is why I say 12+ months as it's not clear exactly how much more than 12 months. Also on the cash they had on hand the team would have likely been earning some kind of yield too.  We also have no clue if ATMTA is earning any other external revenue. They sold Titan ships too and we don't know how much was raised from that. The team has built a lot of Solana tech and had an external revenue deal that had to do with Tokes I believe.  The runway is fine IMO, our current players just need to stop dumping tokens and let the team cook. Team selling tokens means more development which increases the odds of Star Atlas' great success.

author: Space Bull
content:
Can anyone give some insight into this free to play angle we're taking?  I would guess this is in response to small players getting priced out of Sage under the current market conditions, fees, etc.
I would have preferred to see us stay the path despite even myself not playing SAGE for a long time. Maybe someone can make a case that I'm not seeing but it feels like this will continue to overcomplicate things for new players and fracture our player base.  Any thoughts?

author: Space Bull
content: i.e.  Someone who maybe would have bought a couple of crew cards to participate in the shooting games and maybe an X4 or two might just be an F2P player instead.  Therefore not participating in the economy.

author: Space Bull
content:
I suppose one could make the case that person isn't really participating in the economy currently anyways, so maybe they start with F2P and graduate into the "real deal" at some point, ideally.
But I would have thought that working to make the existing game and economy more receptive to these types of players would be a better course of action than splitting the two worlds entirely... if that makes sense.

author: Space Bull
content: I'd also thought that a "rent to own" ship model was intended to solve this at some point.  Again, maybe the current economic conditions don't support someone renting to own say an X4 and then having to play for over a year or something to pay it off and make it their own.  But at least they'd be participating in the same game, same economy as the rest of us.

author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: Can anyone give some insight into this free to play angle we're taking?  I would guess this is in response to small players getting priced out of Sage under the current market conditions, fees, etc.    I would have preferred to see us stay the path despite even myself not playing SAGE for a long time. Maybe someone can make a case that I'm not seeing but it feels like this will continue to overcomplicate things for new players and fracture our player base.  Any thoughts?

reply.preview: Can anyone give some insight into this free to play angle we're taking? Maybe someone can make a case that I'm not seeing but it feels like this will continue to overcomplicate things for new players and fracture our player base. Any thoughts?

author: Space Bull
content: It's a separate ecosystem. A F2P copy of the Solana SAGE. It's meant to get players (who won't initially invest) hooked on the game play (MUCH faster version).  Then brand new players will be more likely to take the plunge into crypto - buying/renting ships, crew, etc in the "real" SAGE layer.  Onboarding strategy. I think it's promising, if done right, with a slick funnel to SAGE.

author: Space Bull
content: Swagner went over it a bit in the spaces he was on yesterday.  The f2p is on their Solana fork/clone and it's completely free.  It's basically a funnel to Sage mainnet. People can get comfortable playing f2p, work on their skills etc, then dive into mainnet when they are ready.  There will be some mainnet rewards like progression and maybe an NFT if you complete the full Holosim experience.  It's all extremely positive.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content: Right on.  Thanks Bryan.  I'll try and check that out today.   Since you mentioned some form of Web 3 reward for the f2p, that at least gives the f2p player some incentive to switch over.    But I'm curious, when you say it gives players a chance to get comfortable, work on their skills etc. before diving in to mainnet, that sounds great and all, but is that really a point of friction that we're seeing?   Like, we'd have a ton more players if they just had a test bed to try things out before jumping in?  I don't know that I've seen or heard that as a common problem or request from newer players.    Of course, I'm not as tapped in recently, so maybe I'm misinformed.

Wait, I can earn something? Let me buy some assets."

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
Ok, it seems a lot of users have the same problem.
Here are the instructions for fixing the claim process in buddylink V1. 
You need a RPC for it (a free Quicknode will do it, you will get some "Too many requests" errors, but the V1 will retry the requests).

F12 -> Sources -> _next/static/chunks/pages/_app-4dcf3a5452194bfd.js 
Right-click on it, select "Override content". Chrome asks you where to save and then you need to allow Chrome the access to the selected folder (a question shows up in the header of the page). Then you can edit the file (double click "_app[..]" in your override window):

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
Find and replace 3x "elwira-tp4ejq-fast-mainnet.helius-rpc.com" with your own RPC (leave the protocol intact, https:// and wss://).
So if your own RPC is e.g. my-qn-rpc.solana-mainnet.quiknode.pro/124839080594309509c, you simply replace the V1 RPC in the line , eS = "https://elwira-tp4ejq-fast-mainnet.helius-rpc.com"
with , eS = "https://my-qn-rpc.solana-mainnet.quiknode.pro/124839080594309509c"
Then do the same with the other 2 RPC lines.
CTRL-S to save. Reload the page, look at the console. An error may show up. Go to the error position (around line 51678) and just delete the complete block inside the catch statement.
So from  "if (console.error(`Received ${ecinstanceof Error ? "" : "JSON-RPC "}error calling `${ea}``, {"
to await this._updateSubscriptions()
so only
catch (ec) {
}
is left.
CTRL-S again, reload and now it should work. You may need to connect your wallet again (click the button on the V1 site). You may have to wait some time until the balanace shows up and you may get a lot of "Too many requests" messages when using a free quicknode RPC).
And you may need to send the claim tx 2 or 3 times before it gets accepted.

author: 《VΛ》| Tango⪛⦿⫺Z.ike |Econ Vantarah
content: The point-system/fleets size depends on your xp/council rank/research tree, I have not heard anything about hangars in the townhall, for fleet assembly: Preset Configurations: Instead of infinite customizability initially, C4 will launch with preset configurations for each ship, typically 15 to 20 per ship. These configurations can be specialized, for example, a "mining config" that optimizes a ship for mining by equipping drills instead of weapons. • Component-Based Assembly: When you add a ship to a fleet, you will select which configuration you want to use. To activate that configuration, you must have the required components, modules, ship weapons, countermeasures, and missiles present in your starbase. These components are distinct items that are slotted into your ship. • Dynamic Stats: Each selected configuration will have completely separate stats, giving the developers full control over how each config affects every ship value. This configurability aims to make combat more dynamic, potentially allowing a strategically configured smaller fleet to defeat a larger, but misconfigured, one.

author: Steven Sabol (Economics)MUD
content: I just don’t like the idea of selling the data, I have been doing things for free because I am passionate about the democracy of data and I believe it goes against the ethos of this project to try to monetize the information. Remember I got into Star atlas and web3 gaming because I was the first star alas econ hire. I’m not trying to open a consulting business I’m trying to provide this economy with economic data that will help it become accepted by traditional investors and economists. I want to change the fundamental idea from this is a game I don’t care about to an economy that must be tracked. That requires a lot of work and a deep understanding of government statistics and financial statistics that I uniquely have, because I spent years updating federal reserve economists on the macro data

author: Steven Sabol (Economics)MUD
content: I also started a economics data firm that collected historical statistics, and I sold that business. I am uniquely positioned to fundamentally change this industry, and open it up to traditional investors.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Definitely, start giving more of the existing NFTs some utility even if that utility changes a bit from its original purpose.  With my idea players can choose Starbases close to the battle lines (maybe not on the battle lines) at the risk of losing them. I would add some staking timers. A timer for the badge to start providing the discount and a timer to unstake. This way it could not be used just before a battle and could not be withdrawn during.  Maybe VIP badges just provide location respawn benefits, and other badges provide respawn timer benefits. Or VIP reduces the respawn timer by a % and other badges depending on the badge level gives you the ability to respawn X amount of regions away from the CSS.    Honestly badges could work more like specialty cards or a get out of jail free cards where you get special powers powered by liquid light or some other resources energy, giving you the ability to only use them so many times, or only so many times within a period of time and then they need to recharge. Better yet to recharge a badges power it would require a recipe of resources like repairing a ship skin. The more rare the badge the more rare the material required to recharge the badge. @SW4GN3R  You could leave all fee discounts in game for ATLAS stakers

Honestly badges could work more like specialty cards or a get out of jail free cards where you get special powers powered by liquid light or some other resources energy, giving you the ability to only use them so many times, or only so many times within a period of time and then they need to recharge. Better yet to recharge a badges power it would require a recipe of resources like repairing a ship skin. The more rare the badge the more rare the material required to recharge the badge. @SW4GN3R You could leave all fee discounts in game for ATLAS stakers

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
It would be very interesting to have wagers to enter a Holosim instance and that entire factions could be eliminated. Last faction standing wins the pot. Fast gameplay, fast matches, with money on the line.

Would be interesting to have smaller maps like in Star Craft for 1v1 or 2v2 wagered matches

Everyone at my work is playing a free to play golf game. Each match has a different wager, sometimes they win, sometimes they lose, but they are grinding to build their balance. They pay $12 per season to level up faster, they can win a free $25 for doing daily challenges (no guarantees) but they always spend it as they feel it's free money even though they could cash out the $25 each time they win.

The video I posted yesterday hit on how a dozen or so of my coworkers think. All very big mobile gamers, sometimes playing for +6 hrs a day

author: [COEX] NDR
content:
What defines a border-line or border-region in C4? Is it the warp-lanes connects regions each other or the adjacent regions (i.e UST-004 and UST-007) are also considered to be border-region?

See picture. UST-007 has warplane to UST-006. So these two would be border-region definitely without question. They are both adjacent and have warplane in between.
How about UST-007 and UST-004? They don't have warplane in between. Lets assume there is an opposing faction who controls UST-004, can they attack UST-007 directly (for taking the region)?

author: SantiMod
content: What defines a border-line or border-region in C4? Is it the warp-lanes connects regions each other or the adjacent regions (i.e UST-004 and UST-007) are also considered to be border-region?  See picture. UST-007 has warplane to UST-006. So these two would be border-region definitely without question. They are both adjacent and have warplane in between. How about UST-007 and UST-004? They don't have warplane in between. Lets assume there is an opposing faction who controls UST-004, can they attack UST-007 directly (for taking the region)?

author: [AEP] Moustacheride
content: @bunthius I don’t disagree with the need to upgrade to higher tier equipment for end game content I guess my concern was only having certain things available behind a 7.5k usdc paywall. If the equipment like the mining drill could be upgraded to tier 7 on a CS T1 and it could get genesium off that that would feel better to me anyway since many players won’t be left out of the opportunity to mine the really rare resource. Perhaps that T1 doesn’t have access to all the bells and whistles the T5 doesn’t but they still have access to the same number of resources.

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I think this might be due to SAGE currently being a hybrid solution — kind of a mirror of what’s happening on-chain, but running on a Google Cloud service.
(Firebase is what made the game feel 100x smoother and faster compared to the early days.)

Maybe yesterday’s outage left some lingering issues.
I noticed that reloading the page — which should force a refresh from on-chain data — seems to mitigate the problem.

At least, that’s how I’ve explained the current situation to myself

content: I really don't understand the demonization of OTC. Literally every MMO has it. Making a micro-tx fee for every little thing is going to kill this game long term.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
I feel like, fleet to fleet (sort of needed to fuel rescues) makes a lot of sense. First the amount is restricted due to fleet size and you could also make it where you have to undocked in space somewhere. Requiring travel time + limited amount transferred making less than ideal.

The other option is making it possible for someone to fill their fleet from someone else’s starbase but they can only deposit to that same persons starbase. Allowing transports

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: there are honestly so many possibilities that could be built into game design, but whenever the topic is brought up, anyone doing OTC is "cheating" apparently, which is bizarre to me.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I understand the label likely doesn't come from ATMTA, but maybe an official statement on OTC and how it may or may not be implemented in the future, could add some clarity to the whole debate. As it is now, many people use OTC, and to label it as "cheating" just seems weird. I get that ATMTA needs to make their revenue, but to try and lobby for OTC as taboo, just seems odd, considering every single MMO i've ever played has both a marketplace and can also do p2p trades.

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: Not to mention that forcing everyone to utilize a local or global marketplace, doesn't sound very decentralized to me.

author: [HEIM] CapThor
content: Figured a "trick". For fleet with as many as it allows you, then remove crew from it. What's happening is that randomly, it's adding ships with more crew than it needs. So, by removing passengers, you allow more ships to be added without going over the fleet's max capacity. Every time you do it, the next time you can add more ships, because total possible crew went up. Hope it helps!

content: I think burn is over used and is a zing/marketing tactic.. max cap (total supply) can effectively do the same but honestly emissions is the big one with lack of “sinks” where sinks here are utilities. With a game where DAU is the whole point a burn can be more harm than good. What if we burn hard and fast but then we explode in numbers and we have to dip into decimals to make it work. Humans hate decimals so we’ll have to do silly things like polkadot or helium and shift decimals or mint more

author: SantiMod
content: I generally agree. It's a gimmick. I've commented on this many times that I'm not a proponent of burning tokens because it's just a destruction of value. People assume that destroying value will lead to it being redistributed in the remaining supply, which is a relationship that hardly ever holds true -- but crypto people love it. Burning tokens implies we need to "steal" value from some stakeholder. It's either value that could go to players, the DAO, or to ATMTA. Somebody in that equation has to lose if we implement a burn. For example, we could burn a portion of all crafting fees in the game, but that implies the DAO is receiving less revenue as a result.  To achieve growth in the value of ATLAS, we need to see player growth, and the incentives for saving (sinks) need to be large enough to encourage players to compound their earnings and lock them instead of just using ATLAS as exit liquidity. Marketplace fee discounts are one of those incentives (which is also why OTC hurts the ecosystem, since OTC traders have less incentive to lock tokens -- not trying to demonize them, just stating facts). Game complexity and specialization is another attribute that will drive value for savings, because it should lead more trade between players, which is inherently exclusively denominated in ATLAS in game, and therefore, players will need to maintain some balance of ATLAS to facilitate trade. And this is also why we introduced the ATLAS Locker Addendum - not as a way to incorporate pay-to-win, which certainly isn't our goal, but to further encourage savings. But the combination of QOL improvements, higher ROI of game assets, and native yield from the lockers themselves are intended to be a motivating factor for saving ATLAS (in the locker).  I think the biggest misconception the community has is that ATLAS is inflationary and POLIS is not. That's not true. They're currently both fixed supply assets, following very similar emission (inflation) curves. They're both inflationary, to a point. The only caveat here is that there is a strong possibility that permanent/perpetual inflation will be necessary on ATLAS at the end of the emission curve to provide future game subsidies to players -- though it's not inconceivable that P2P trade creates enough value in the economy in the future to avoid that perpetual inflation rate.  Final point, I did tease that we would potentially be interested in burning ATLAS used in microtransactions in Holosim. That would result in ATMTA sacrificing revenue for the burn mechanic. But because I believe adoption in Holosim could scale very rapidly, and we have new opportunities for web2 style microtransaction monetization. So, I'm potentially amenable to burning that new revenue stream because the volume of burn could be substantial and could lead to price improvement in ATLAS, which benefits the entire ecosystem (higher profitability, more assets sales, etc.)

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Probably important to clarify that C4 isn't necessarily going to "prevent inflation." Perhaps to some degree, because we are moving fully back to the LP system (with new ways of earning LP). But the result is a fixed daily emission schedule. That only differs from Based in that technically FICs create unlimited potential for emissions, only gated by resource production and requirements. What's more important, and where the tokenomics come into play, is the extent to which players feel compelled to maintain balances of ATLAS for game activity. Which I alluded to in my post above.  Long term, the real path to a sustainable economy relies on non-extractive capital entering the game. Which is to say, players that are willing to spend money to play  (for entertainment) and that don't care about profitability. They're just consumers. The reason this works for us is because, by design, ATMTA has defined those as revenue streams to players. i.e. ATMTA is not a seller of food or fuel. The player economy produces those items and other players will transact within the P2P economy for them. So, instead of paying ATMTA $10/month to play, they might spend $10/month on fuel from other players and be happy with that dynamic.

content: Probably important to clarify that C4 isn't necessarily going to "prevent inflation." Perhaps to some degree, because we are moving fully back to the LP system (with new ways of earning LP). But the result is a fixed daily emission schedule. That only differs from Based in that technically FICs create unlimited potential for emissions, only gated by resource production and requirements. What's more important, and where the tokenomics come into play, is the extent to which players feel compelled to maintain balances of ATLAS for game activity. Which I alluded to in my post above.  Long term, the real path to a sustainable economy relies on non-extractive capital entering the game. Which is to say, players that are willing to spend money to play  (for entertainment) and that don't care about profitability. They're just consumers. The reason this works for us is because, by design, ATMTA has defined those as revenue streams to players. i.e. ATMTA is not a seller of food or fuel. The player economy produces those items and other players will transact within the P2P economy for them. So, instead of paying ATMTA $10/month to play, they might spend $10/month on fuel from other players and be happy with that dynamic.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: @Narr_nEoN - ⁠😎┃foundation-room⁠  It's a pretty difficult problem to solve but I haven't stopped thinking about how to "combat" this behavior. Sadly limiting based on users (wallets) is pretty easy to work around since wallets are free and can be easily scripted. It does appear that they're shotgunning accept_rental to snag them when they become available.  This will become even easier once the SDK is released and as such is the top of my list. One answer is to make it available to everyone (but that sort of fights fire with fire). Another answer is to increase the supply of fleets on the market (but that requires users to use the rental system). Another answer is to implement some sort of crowdbased market valuation tool. Since a lot of people are annoyed at these bots getting the best deals. If all the deals were "equal" the bots will be paying the same as everyone else. At which point, if it still happens it tells me that there's more demand than supply at hand as well.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: As it is today that's true but I've been wanting to implement the ability to transfer an already rented fleet. The original borrower will maintain the rental controls (think cancellations) but the new borrower would have control over the fleet upon transfer. Having this in place will help people when they rent to the wrong subprofile or DACs in lending rentals to guildee's but will make it easy to spin up burner wallets to rent fleets and then simply move them to the operating wallet unfortunately.

author: [SH]b0b [STAR]
content:
Meta changed a bit, earlier people was pissed coz bots took best mining fleets. Now there only fiew really OP options through all 3 factions (ship lords adjusted prices) so it's not a big deal, also you cant infinitely rent mining ships if you don't have crew/transport etc, that's where demand was important. Now bots picking every scanning fleet which give you above 0 atlas profit. I'm tired of this holy war, but nothing except any sort of auction will help here, unfortunately. I totally agree with @Narr_nEoN current rental system help fiew people in our community (who have alot of crew and lack of mining), but mostly helps bot builders to extract atlas

author: Definitely_Not_JayWalk
content:
We are pretty much in the death spiral phase right now.  A few people have monopolized the Fleet rental program and there are some major extrators that continually dump tokens.  Not really sure there is a way out as this is the same exact pattern that happens to all web3 games...just took us longer to get here.  No way anybody looking from an external view point would want to get in here (in terms of investing real money) with the way things are and not sure how we survive without new players.  Thus the death spiral continues.

Would love to hear some ideas that counter the above.  Obviously new gamplay would help, but not sure if that is enough at this point as overall optics are not great.

author: The Man | RomeONI
content: Is the team still selling tokens at the same rate with prices this low? Would it make sense to reduce the amount sold until the price is higher, for a larger return and longer runway? If we are near the end of the runway it makes sense to sell at full speed of course, but if not, the better long term play seems to be selling while prices are higher. And of course not adding to the impression of a "Death Spiral" as Jaywalk put it. Ship prices and everything else depends on atlas price, and while I personally don't care for the actual price of the token, the economy being built needs it to not tank. Lowering the downward pressure when it's already going down may be a good idea. 2 separate thoughts on the same thing.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content:
Just stopping by to add to the debate. I think the TK problem affects Ustur more than the other factions due to some factors.

The ONIs don't care much about this situation for 2 simple reasons:
1 - They are the faction with the most advanced bases, so there are few spaces to install TKs;
2 - They are totally focused on the T5 arms race, so the LP will always be bad for TK snipers.

The MUD is heading in a similar direction, but a little more controlled.

Ustur has been suffering from this for months (literally). According to my research, since the first month that the FICs arrived and the biggest Ustur whales started to focus more on them, Ustur's overall LP has fallen slightly, and it only didn't fall more because some players took the opportunity to set up a TK setup throughout the Ustur safezone, keeping the TK candles lit for weeks without interruption.
This scenario was not replicated in any other faction.

Adding an extra layer, I made an estimated calculation of, if at that moment the TKS of all starbases were activated 24/7, how much would the impact be on the LP of each faction:
ONI - 133.488.000 TK/day = 1.86BI LP
MUD - 116.802.000 TK/day = 1.63BI LP
USTUR - 143.499.600 TK/day = 2.00BI LP

author: [HEIM] SDU pimpUstur
content: I’ve got a fairly balanced fleet, been one of if not the largest sdu scanner in mud for many months. Ive calculated the cost and profit of every non marketplace related loop including sol fees, fuel costs, and food/ammo burn. Sdu is by far the worst in terms of atlas income. Base upkeep sniping via tk for example is the second worst, fics are better than some component deposits but worse than most given average lp. Obviously it depends on your fleet composition, but for most users they would have the same results.   I wouldn’t call tk sniping meta by any means, but it is certainly better than sdus.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: @ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics I can walk you through it. There’s no risk to ships, or components/resources, but there is a risk to xp and crew.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: Two risks specifically. One is the inherent risk of using a hot wallet which can be largely mitigated by using a clean computer, the other risk is making a mistake by using the wrong address which can be mitigated by simulating the process on a test account for practice, or just not copying the wrong address.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: Btw I’ve done it 3 times, 6 if you include the practice runs. Once for me and twice walking others through it.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: I’m fairly certain there’s a safer, easier solution involving permissioned hot wallets. But I haven’t done it. Would be easy to check if it’s possible given available tools tho, and if it is would be easy to do in theory, but it’s uncharted waters so no promises.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: My server creates an optimized SDU chance map, SLYA requests the data and each fleet decides where to scan next. Fleets can now even interrupt the scan cooldown and move on when the chance of the current sector drops below a certain threshold (e.g. because another fleet found SDUs in the sector). Also fleets can use the way back to the starbase to find additional SDUs. 3 weeks of research and work was needed for it.    oh, and to keep SLYA free, I pay the server costs. Groove only updates the repo now and then (and also merges my patches then). If you want to use the latest features, this is my repo/branch with all the latest patches/features/fixes:  https://github.com/Swift42/SLY-Assistant/tree/patch-collection-for-0.7.0

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Even if the Holosim doesn't use the same economics as Starbased or C4, it can be used as a testing ground for those products. Especially improvements to the underlying tech of ATLASNET and battle testing our Blockchain code.   We can't draw a direct comparison of the economy between Starbased and Holosim, but we can study the economic relief Holosim provides because it's running on a fork of Solana. We can study the impact high risk zones would create, and balance combat mechanics months ahead of the C4 PTR.   We're also seeing an incredibly sticky game with Holosim, and that is a very good sign considering there's no monetary incentive whatsoever, and it's built using the exact same building blocks as C4.   And finally, I've seen first hand how awesome it's been for players to participate in closed play testing. Holosim is us taking every opportunity to include as many of our players in game development as possible and providing a really fun experience at the same time.   Cc @ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics

author: Xcode [STAR]
content: Thanks for the insight sir.   I agree all of that is valuable but I really worry that modeling any economics or player gameplay based on what players do in a free environment is dangerous. Player behavior is going to be about 100000x different then on Mainnet where there are literal economic repercussions for everything.  Treating Holosim like a stand-alone game and monetizing THAT (e.g. microtx) feels pretty good.  Also slightly worrying if Holosim is too good then players may not want to come to Mainnet. Did anyone think of that?     For example things that wont translate... (again my opinions)  In Holosim players are crafting large and capital ships and suiciding them into enemy factions to try and disrupt mining operations.  Will that translate into Mainnet?, Modeling HRZ in Holosim, I can't see any correlation to Mainnet where HRZ will have permanent destruction., Holosim is giving free rewards every day, e.g. crew.  (21 crew at 5 each is $105 value per day, not viable on mainnet), Battle testing blockchain code.  50/50 here. Mainnet is totally different. Priority fees and complex multiple transactions are wildly different on mainnet., Anything where there is an economic incentive to do something., Things that do translate (again my opinion).  Technical bits like players intercepting other players via subwarp, or when warp ends., Quest system in general.  Daily rewards are awesome. Will they still be sticky when you aren't giving away $100 in crew every day?, Blockchain Code (mostly, im sure things will still break on mainnet),

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: I agree that they are independent "games" (although will be integrative within the SA universe lore), and that there are a lot of cases in which they will never reconcile from a mechanic or incentive standpoint. But there are going to be areas where we can extract knowledge, and Holosim is a great testing ground given the inconsequential nature of gameplay (losing a ship in Holosim isn't the same as losing a ship in SAGE).  And yes, we've thought about whether or not Holosim could cannibalize mainnet features. I personally think that's pretty low risk. In a lot of ways, I think they're complementary. Holosim is something you could engage in in-between establishing logistical operations (slower) in SAGE/FC. I think there will be may players who only ever play Holosim, many who only ever play SAGE, and some proportion that engage in both. But nothing quite has the allure of a legitimate real-cash economy. And if Holosim helps drive revenues, that just means we have more resources to pursue the larger vision of Star Atlas.

author: 영양주사Ustur
content: Hi, I'm a person who sees the future of Star Atlas positively and dreams of becoming Ready Player One in Star Atlas. I'm also the owner of the Commandership. I have two questions. One is, I hope the combat system doesn't just use far-range attacks. Like the Star Wars Squadrons (Video: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=zP4LyaOLD0A&pp=ygUZ7Jqw7KO86rKM7J6EIOyKpO2DgOybjOymiA%3D%3D) I hope they can also introduce a close combat system. It's in the development stage, but ultimately it's doing that, right? And in the future, if you don't authenticate your personal information to the state, you'll be caught in money laundering and tax evasion, and the state will suppress you until the Star Atlas game. You're considering KYC, right?

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
How about giving the FIC system a bit of a rework?
Since you’ve already made production harder by increasing time requirements, maybe the crafting fees could be reduced a little.
Right now, the fees feel a bit unfair — for example, crafting 100k units costs 35,600 in crafting fees, plus 12,000 for listing and another 5,000–6,000 in Solana fees.
And that’s not even counting the item gathering and transport costs. On top of that, it takes a lot of time.
I really think the fees for Copper, Copper Wire, Electromagnet, and PA in the FIC should be lowered.
Especially considering how much Atlas has been losing value compared to Solana lately.
Hopefully you’ll consider adjusting this, so we can actually produce and mint FICs in a sustainable way.
